chat_log.content:
I see you're a new user — your profile shows the initial interview hasn't been completed yet.

Before we do anything else, I want to spend about 20 minutes getting an accurate picture of who you are and what you're up against — not the elevator pitch version, the honest one. I'll ask about your background, what you're building, and what typically gets in your way. From that I'll calibrate how I coach you. There are no right answers. The less polished your answers are, the more useful this will be. Goals come after this — first I want to understand the person.

Let's start with your background.

**What did you do before this?** Not the LinkedIn version — what did the work actually involve, and how long did you do it?

i started as a motor mechanic, then did production engineering, then trained as a software developer, but I've never had real qualifications and so I can never apply for 'real' jobs. So I've moved around from indie mobile app development to web building, then did some product documenting and called myself a 'conversational architect' while building out some chatbot scripts back when chatbots had scripts to follow.  Somehow I've been doing this for over 10 years

Good — that's a real answer. Mechanic to production engineering to self-taught developer to mobile apps to web to chatbots. Ten-plus years of building things across different domains, all without the formal credentials that would let you walk into a "real" job interview.

That's a particular kind of path — you've had to prove competence through output instead of credentials, over and over.

**What made you move through all those transitions?** Was each shift a deliberate choice, or did something push you — a job ending, an opportunity appearing, getting bored, hitting a ceiling?

trained mechanic to mechanical/production engineer because I felt I wasn't done with education, so I paid for it myself.  Then needed to earn some money while at university so worked as a sound engineer evenings and weekends. Then moved into sound system installations, control systems, more programming, then wanted to live abroad so went remote and mobile, more and more coding.  Really love languages so leaned toward voice-first but never found my true calling or felt strongly enough about something to fully focus, so have ended up being dragged from project to project developing other people's ideas instead of my own. Now feel I understand agentic development better than most, but am still failing to actually create anything useful so cannot 'prove' my competence, and am watching less able people earning big because they just happen to be in the right job.

That's a clear picture. Self-funded education, multiple pivots driven by curiosity and circumstance, a real facility with languages and voice-first work, and a pattern of building other people's ideas while your own stay on the sideline.

And now you're watching people with less capability earn more because they're positioned where you're not — while you're sitting on understanding you can't point to as evidence.

The phrase that stands out: *"never found my true calling or felt strongly enough about something to fully focus."*

I want to push on that a little.

**Is it that you haven't found something worth focusing on — or that you've avoided committing fully to something because it might not work, and then you'd have real evidence that it failed?**

Not an accusation. A genuine question. Because those two things feel similar from the inside but they're very different problems.

current areas of interest include 1) nutrition (due to being overweight and unable to reduce it) but despite very indepth knowledge have not proved it personally works due to unknown issues) and I am unable to show authority in these areas, so building something would be confronted with "who are you to make these claims?"  2) language acquisition, again I have lots of knowledge and understanding, but have not personally learned 5 languages, have no qualifications so no authority in this domain.  Other areas have experience but due to jumping around I feel insufficient, or not recent enough to gain credibility

You didn't answer the question I asked — and that's useful information.

I asked whether the issue is not finding something worth focusing on, or avoiding commitment because failure would be visible. You responded with a list of domains and why you can't claim authority in any of them.

That's a pattern worth naming: **you're pre-disqualifying yourself before you start.** Each domain has a reason you can't be credible there. Nutrition — haven't proven it on yourself. Languages — haven't learned five of them. Other areas — not recent enough, not deep enough.

The common thread isn't the domains. It's the belief that you need to be an unimpeachable authority before you can build anything.

Here's the thing: you have 10+ years of building things across multiple technical domains. You understand agentic development "better than most" — your words. You've been a conversational architect, a developer, a systems thinker. That's not nothing. That's a lot.

**What skills or judgment from all those years — mechanic, engineer, developer, voice-first work — do you find yourself using constantly in the new work, even if you wouldn't call them by their old names?**

I'm asking what you're actually good at, underneath the domain labels.

i think i am good at logically understanding a dynamic system.  For example, in language learning the typical approach is to memorise vocab from books, but this is not how language works and I have studied how language is built up from sounds to syllables to breath group phrases which give meaning, not from trying to piece vocab together using learned grammar and syntax.  Another example is nutrition, where nutritionist are taught standard foods groups and then they can tell people what diet to eat, but I have taken it much further breaking each category into even more detailed categories.  Take fruit as an example, most nutritions would just say "eat fruit, its good for you" but I say is is citric (no sugar) tree fruit (apples, pears etc.. are medium slow release sugar) berries, (concentrated sugar) etc..  Same with meat, same with veg (is it root, stem, buld, stalks, leaves or flower). There's are whole lot more, but when I describe it to people the label me as opinionated because it does not fit their accepted narrative.  So while I am good at abstracting dynamic systems, it seems to me that's not very useful when the audience is not interested

Now we're getting somewhere real.

You're a systems thinker who goes deeper than the conventional frameworks — you break categories into their underlying mechanics. Sounds to syllables to breath groups. Citric versus tree fruit versus berries. Root, stem, bulb, leaf, flower. You see the structure beneath the labels.

And when you share that, people call you "opinionated" because it doesn't fit their existing mental model.

So you've concluded: *this skill isn't useful because the audience isn't interested.*

But that's not quite right. The skill is useful — it's rare, actually. What's missing is the **translation layer**. You're presenting the deep structure to people who are still operating at the surface level, and expecting them to follow you down. They can't. Not because they're dumb, but because you haven't built the bridge.

The people earning big that you mentioned earlier — they're often worse at the systems thinking but better at the translation. They meet people where they are.

That's a learnable skill. And it's one where your technical background — explaining complex systems to non-technical people, which you've done across multiple domains — actually applies.

Let me ask the next background question:

**When you describe what you do now to someone from one of your previous fields — say, another developer or engineer — how do you explain it? And what do you usually leave out?**

for the past few years I have trying to find a niche where I can work without too much interaction, trying to build my own products without having to explain these things to people who do want to hear my theories.  When I explained my idea of a digital twin, modelling the human body at a fat storage abstraction level, the nutritionist said it was too complicated for them to understand, and yet the abstraction buried the complexity and surfaced simplicity (in my eyes), another nutritionist told me the body didn't work the way I described it, because that's not the way the nutritionist bible teaches them.  When I spoke to a language teacher they felt threatened and explained at length why my babbling engine would not work, despite it being designed around sounds and syllables like a baby learning their first language, again because it did not fit into the 'accepted' framework, which ironically does not work.  So i find myself not mentioning much of this to anyone, because the feedback is not positive, its their idea of how it should function, a bit like doing a project for someone else but without being paid for it.  Its a struggle

So you've stopped explaining. You've gone into a kind of creative isolation — building without interaction because the interaction has been consistently dismissive or co-opting.

That makes sense as a protective move. But it's also a trap. You're building in the dark, which means:
- No feedback loop to sharpen the work
- No evidence that it works for anyone but you
- No external accountability to finish anything
- No audience forming around the ideas

The nutritionist who said it was "too complicated" and the one who said "that's not how the body works" — they're not your audience. The language teacher who felt threatened — not your audience either. You've been showing deep systems work to people invested in the shallow frameworks. Of course they pushed back.

But "I'll just build alone and not explain it to anyone" isn't the answer. It's the avoidance of finding the *right* audience — people who are frustrated with the standard frameworks, who want the deeper structure, who would actually value what you see.

I'm hearing a clear pattern forming here. Let me ask one more background question before we move on:

**Is there anything about your previous work — mechanic, engineer, developer, any of it — that you're genuinely glad to be done with? And anything you actually miss?**

I am glad to be done with projects that included office politics, the restricted view of clients who ask for functionality restricted by their own narrow, limited understanding of the possible solutions.  The domain expert that steps out of his area into the solutions area and tries to dictate instead of providing domain expertise.  I actually miss the hands on problem-solving of previous vehicle diagnostic work, the psychological aspects of divide and succeed, hours of trying to understand and finally finding the cause.  I miss working with sound reinforcement systems where I fully understand how everything is connected, and within seconds of seeing a problem, understanding how to fix it.  I still claim my best job was working in a Spanish hostel bumbling along in English, Spanish, French but switching between them at a moments notice.

That's a vivid contrast.

**What drained you:** Office politics, clients who constrain solutions to their limited imagination, domain experts who overstep into telling you *how* to build instead of *what* they need.

**What lit you up:** Hands-on diagnostic problem-solving — the psychological hunt, the hours of working toward the root cause. Systems you fully understood where you could see the problem and know the fix instantly. And that hostel job — switching between languages fluidly, in real time, in service of real human connection.

There's a thread there: you come alive when you're *inside* a system you deeply understand, solving real problems in real time, with autonomy over how you solve them. The moment someone else's limited framework gets imposed on you — whether it's a client, a domain expert, or a credentialing system — something dies.

That's a good stopping point for your background. I have a clear picture now: where you came from, how long you were in it, why you're here, and what energizes versus drains you.

**We can stop here and pick up next time with your current build and goals, or we can keep going now.** The next section is about what you're actually building — what exists, what you've shipped, who it's for. Maybe another 15 minutes.

What do you want to do?
we can keep going, but due to agentic coding there is more than 1 project build, or should I say more than 1 project fail.  Should I pick one?

Yes — pick one. The one you think about most right now, even if it's stalled or failed.

We're not looking for the most impressive or the most likely to succeed. We're looking for the one that has the most grip on your attention, for whatever reason.

**What are you building? Describe it as it actually is right now — not what it will be, what it is today.**

I am building a babbling engine which takes the users native language, maps it at a sound, syllable and breath-group phrase level to any number of selected languages they show an interest in acquiring.  The human pysiology can only produce a total of around 50-60 sounds, so many languages use the same sounds and syllables, but in different sequences, different phonology and frequency of use.  So if a child can make themselves understood with 300 words and a good playful understanding of their language, the babbling engine allows adults to do this by stacking multiple languages and playing with their commonalities all at once, its not language->sound, its sound->languages.  The app was built using agentic framework, but as with all of them, it starts out fine but soon becomes a slop factory, then when something does not work as intended its really hard to fix.  The flutter app runs, allows language selection, installs onnx but does not actually make any sounds when the user taps play. It's been a short while since I looked at the code, it's another abandoned agentic codebase

Good. That's a real description of something real.

A babbling engine that inverts the standard model — sound to languages, not language to sounds. Built on the insight that human physiology produces a limited sound set, and languages share more than they differ at that level. The idea is to let adults acquire language the way children do: through playful sound-making, not vocabulary memorization.

The app exists. It runs. Language selection works, ONNX installs. But when you tap play — silence. And because it was built with an agentic framework that degraded into slop, you can't easily fix it. So it sits there, abandoned.

That's the honest state of it.

**What have you shipped?** Not on this project necessarily — anything. A landing page, a prototype someone else used, a tool you gave to one person, a previous version of this. What exists outside your head or your hard drive that someone else has touched?
if you are asking for a 20 year project history you might be overstretching yourself, what is the real question about, are you asking about my technical capabilities or ability to ship?

Fair push. I'm asking about your ability to ship.

Specifically: **has anyone else ever used something you built?** Not whether you *can* build — that's clear. Whether you've put something in front of a real person and let them interact with it.

One example is enough.

If I take away project contributions in a team, and unfinished projects I have jumped between trying to get working, I would not be able to think of a single project I would pull up in front of a potential client and be proud to demonstrate to them,  there is no portfolio and I do not know why that is the case.  I know all about proving the idea before building but always seem to want to get to a point that proves to myself I can do it, as well as to the client that it can be done before I ship.  I am good at starting, just like agentic coding is good at the start, but not so good at finishing, or even getting far enough

That's an honest answer, and it's important.

You've built for teams, you've started many things, you understand the *principle* of shipping early — but when it comes to something that's yours, with your name on it, that a client or user could see and judge... nothing has crossed that line.

And you named the mechanism: *"I always seem to want to get to a point that proves to myself I can do it, as well as to the client that it can be done before I ship."*

That's the trap. You're trying to pre-prove competence before exposing the work to anyone who could evaluate it. Which means the work never gets exposed. Which means there's no portfolio. Which means you can't prove competence. Loop closed.

This is a pattern I'm going to name more precisely later. For now, I want to note it and keep moving.

**The babbling engine — who is it for?** Not a market segment. One specific person who would actually use this. Someone you can picture. Do they know it exists?
its for me, and anyone else who knows traditional methods don't work

"It's for me, and anyone else who knows traditional methods don't work."

That's honest, but it's also vague enough to be unfindable. "Anyone who knows traditional methods don't work" is a belief, not a person. You can't send them an email. You can't watch them try to use it.

Let me ask it differently:

**Is there one real person — someone you actually know or have interacted with — who has expressed frustration with traditional language learning and might genuinely want what you're building?**

Not a hypothetical user. A name, or at least a face you can picture.
